name: Lu and Wang Real Student MCQ Comparison Extract
population_or_context: Multiple-choice question item evaluation for Cambridge International AS and A Level Psychology questions.
sample_size: 45 GPT-4 simulated student profiles answer 20 multiple-choice questions, with comparison to real student responses in the same item-evaluation setting.
methods: Extracted the real-response comparison elements for GPT-4 simulated student profiles, including profile-rule response alignment, real-response correlation, and difficult-item overlap.
education_context: Psychology multiple-choice question item evaluation in an assessment-design support workflow.
response_origin: GPT-4-simulated student profiles generate multiple-choice responses used as preliminary item-evaluation evidence.
rubric_label_space: Multiple-choice answer options and profile rules for mastered, confused, and unknown knowledge components.
label_source: Generated profiles and responses provide simulated signals; real student responses provide the comparator for item-level behavior.
generation_method: LLM-simulated student profiles answer multiple-choice questions under profile-conditioned prompting.
prompt_strategy: Profile examples, instructor role prediction prompts, and one-question-at-a-time prompting are used in the reported prompt architecture.
evaluation_method: Profile-response alignment checks, correlation with real student responses, and difficult-item overlap checks.
scorer_use: Question item evaluation support; not scorer training or automated scorer validation.
real_response_comparator: Real student responses to the same item-evaluation setting are used for correlation and difficult-item overlap checks.
reported_metrics: Table 3 reports percent correct, percent matching the profile confusion, and most common simulated answer for mastered, confused, and unknown profile rules.
limitations: The comparison is preliminary item-evaluation evidence, not a replacement for real student testing.
The task is multiple-choice psychology item evaluation, not open-response scoring.
The source reports useful real-response alignment signals but not a complete validation of synthetic student substitutes.
